items.title: Image classifier — train/val accuracy curves
items.scenario: A team training ResNet logs train/accuracy and val/accuracy every epoch. By epoch 40, train accuracy hits 99% while val/accuracy plateaus at 82% from epoch 25 onward.
items.explanation: The diverging curves signal overfitting. Metric tracking makes the plateau visible early— they roll back to the epoch-25 checkpoint instead of training to epoch 100.

items.title: Tabular regression — RMSE on train and val
items.scenario: A pricing model logs train/rmse and val/rmse after each sklearn fit on increasing tree depth. Depth 8 gives val/rmse 1200; depth 12 drops train/rmse to 400 but val/rmse rises to 1350.
items.explanation: Validation RMSE is the selection metric; train RMSE alone would wrongly favor depth 12. Consistent naming lets them pick depth 8 from logged scalars.

items.title: Imbalanced fraud detection — F1 not accuracy
items.scenario: A fraud team logs val/accuracy (99.2%) and val/f1 (0.41) each epoch. Accuracy looks fine because negatives dominate; F1 exposes poor recall on fraud.
items.explanation: Tracking the right metric matters— they designate val/f1 as the primary scalar for model selection and ignore misleading val/accuracy in leaderboards.

items.title: LLM fine-tune — step-level train loss
items.scenario: Fine-tuning logs train/loss every 50 gradient steps and val/loss every 500 steps on a held-out prompt set. A spike at step 1200 correlates with a bad learning-rate warmup change.
items.explanation: Step-granular train loss catches instability within an epoch; less frequent val logging keeps evaluation cost manageable while still producing a usable curve.

items.title: Final test metric logged once
items.scenario: After selecting the best checkpoint by val/f1, the team runs one evaluation on the locked test set and logs test/f1=0.68 with tag final_report=true— no test/f1 curve across epochs.
items.explanation: Test metrics belong in the release record, not the live training dashboard— naming test/f1 distinctly and logging it once preserves an honest sign-off score.

items.title: Hyperparameter sweep comparison
items.scenario: Twenty learning rates each produce a val/loss curve. The experiment tracker stores runs as lr=1e-3, lr=1e-4 with identical metric keys so the UI overlays val/loss curves.
items.explanation: Shared naming conventions (same val/loss key, different run hyperparameters) are what make cross-run scalar comparison possible.
